concerns.text: The plan cites 'https://huggingface.co/datasets/Indic-Benchmark/hindi-arc-c-2.5k/...' as the primary source for ARC-Bench failure transcripts. This dataset contains question-answer pairs for Indic languages, not the 'AutoResearchClaw' failure transcripts (error logs, pivot actions, resolution traces) required to test the hypothesis. The plan admits the dataset lacks 'error_log' columns and proposes synthesizing them or simulating the baseline. Using a QA dataset to synthesize failure logs for a study on 'deterministic rule extraction vs. probabilistic context retrieval' fundamentally changes the research object from 'agent failure modes' to 'synthetic error injection,' invalidating the comparison against a real baseline agent.

The mitigation strategy to 'simulate failure transcripts by injecting known error patterns' or 'generate a synthetic failure dataset' means the core experimental data (failure logs) is not derived from the cited dataset but from the researcher's own generation. This breaks the link between the cited resource and the empirical claim. The study becomes a simulation of a simulation, not an analysis of real agent failures from ARC-Bench, making the 'Time-to-Pivot' and 'Success Rate' metrics unrepresentative of the actual problem space.

The plan states the baseline metrics will be derived from 'a simulated baseline that approximates the latency and success rate of a probabilistic agent.' Since the baseline agent (AutoResearchClaw) is not actually run on the data (due to resource constraints), and the input data (failure logs) is synthetic, the entire comparative study lacks empirical grounding. The 'interaction term' in the regression will measure the properties of the simulation, not the viability of rule extraction on real agent failures.
